classified.text: I understand completely where you’re coming from. Here’s what I would do if I was in your shoes.  1) slow down!! Breath!! People who struggle with ROCD (sounds like you are) ruminate extensively because it’s a copping mechanism. At your core, or in my experience, you’re trying to foresee the future and alienate out any possible “red flags” or signs that this relationship is going to hurt you, and by doing so, you are essentially ruining any chance at having a healthy, open-minded, forgiving and curious thought process.  2) the perfect person doesn’t exist. If you’re trying to sniff out the perfect partner it won’t happen because there is no such thing as a soulmate or perfect person. Soulmates are made not found.  I highly recommend the You Love and You Learn podcast on Spotify. Creator also has struggled with ROCD, overthinking, and relationship perfectionism and talks extensively on how to cope with this type of thinking/anxiety.  3) once you are in a better headspace genuinely asses these doubts and fears, figure out where it’s coming from, and if you still feel inclined to, consider if there’s are non-negotiables in your relationship or something you can work through and compromise on with your partner. Since it’s your first relationship I’d tell you that a dealbreaker is something that if you did it, you would understand if the person wanted to break up with you as well.   At the end of the day remember this is can be unlearned and rewired. You are capable, so hold yourself accountable, forgive yourself for the mistakes you do make, and strive to be better!

classified.text: Hearing a sample from one of my favorite songs in the "wild" reminds me of how cool it is that my favorite artists listened to the same stuff as me — I was listening to the album Stratosfear by Tangerine Dream today. I don't listen to a lot of Tangerine Dream, but when I do it's usually Phaedra or Rubycon, so this was a bit of a new experience. On the song "[Invisible Limits](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Euhwq1IGWIk&t=220s)" around 3:40-ish, there's a couple really good slow paced guitar licks. Their riffs are heavy but funky, the vocals have these unexpected harmonies, and they weren’t afraid of odd time signatures—they really sort of had their own sound, that is just pure fun lol.  But they dropped right as grunge exploded, got zero radio love, and kind of vanished.  Zombies from hell ended up being their most popular song:  [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5MRTsCEHVoY](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5MRTsCEHVoY)  It’s crazy how timing and trends can bury a band that clearly had talent. classified.text: Well, the current last.fm "play your recommendations" is linked to spotify, so maybe you're right? Last.fm has gone through phases of no streaming, streaming, and partner streaming, and TBH I haven't used last.fm as a stream source in quite a while. I guess it seems possible that if they outsource their recommendation playback to spotify, you'll get spotify recommendations. Outside of the spotify integration, last.fm doesn't have visibility into anything that isn't scrobbled AFAIK. It's based on user data only. You have "neighbors" who have similar tastes, which I think is calculated based on overlapping scrobbles (not sure if time-weighted, or just top listens). If we both start scrobbling with a limited amount of artists, and 75% of our scrobbles are the band Primus, we're probably going to be neighbors. If I decide that Primus sucks and start listening to Coldplay all day, our venn diagram overlap separates and we're not neighbors anymore. Maybe the neighbors influence the recommendations, but playback is outsourced to spotify? I guess I don't really know. You can still browse neighbors though, and use their top lists as "recommendations", which should only be based on listening history.

classified.text: I have mixed feelings. Spotify's great for listeners, but as an artist, it kind of feels like you're just feeding content into a machine and hoping it picks it up.  I shifted away from seeing spotify as the goal and treating it more like one of many places people can hear my music. I still pitch to playlists through Submithub, Groover, and Dailyplaylists (free credits help), and yes sometimes small placements actually bring in real listeners, but I don’t expect miracles from it.  The big shift was setting up a website. It gave me a space where I set the rules. I’ve got my tracks, a bio, blog posts about what I’m working on, and most importantly, a mailing list. That mailing list became one of the only ways I could actually reach people who care, without relying on algorithms. It also helped with Google, people searching for my name or my style actually find me now, which didn’t happen before. I'd recommend Noiseyard, easy to set up.  So for me, spotify’s part of the picture, but not the whole plan.

classified.text: Izna announces June comeback as second guest on revamped Weekly Idol"  The girl group izna's first variety show is the idol representative variety show guide 'Weekly Idol.'  On the 19th, as a result of OSEN's coverage, izna (Mai, Bang Ji-min, Yoon Ji-yoon, Coco, Yoo Sa-rang, Choi Jung-eun, Jung Se-bi) has recently completed the recording for the MBC M·MBC Every1 variety show 'Weekly Idol.'  'Weekly Idol' is a flagship variety program of MBC M·MBC Every1 that has been broadcast since July 2011. It is regarded as the variety show guide for all idols in South Korea and the go-to guide for idol fans worldwide, known as a must-do program before idols make a comeback, and is highly popular among fans. In particular, 'Weekly Idol's' 'random play dance' and 'n times speed dance' have garnered attention, establishing the program as an essential course for idols to master.  After concluding its broadcast in December of last year, 'Weekly Idol' is taking a break for reorganization and will return after six months with a new MC. The new MCs will be Monsta X's Min-hyuk and Cravity's Hyung-jun, and the revamped 'Weekly Idol' plans to expand music-centered content while maintaining its existing strengths and enhancing communication with fans. It aims to provide opportunities for direct interaction with fans in spaces where they can communicate through music, establishing a stage where idols and fans can connect even more closely.  izna will appear as the second guest on 'Weekly Idol.' izna is a girl group formed through the survival show 'I-LAND 2,' and the team name embodies bold confidence and conviction, indicating that it can be defined as 'me' at any time, anywhere, and in any situation. By releasing their first mini album 'N/a' on November 25 of last year, they have challenged themselves to break past their limits and fears towards a new world.  With their debut album, izna entered the top 10 on the iTunes top album charts in 14 countries worldwide and achieved 1st place in the K-pop album rankings on Apple Music Japan and Rakuten Music. On March 31, they made a rapid comeback with their first digital single 'SIGN,' which climbed to high positions on major domestic music sites like Melon and Genie right after its release, and also ranked 2nd on Spotify Japan's rising chart, 2nd on Amazon Music's singles popularity, and 3rd on iTunes K-Pop top songs, showcasing their prominence in major Japanese charts. They celebrated winning first place on their debut music program SBS M 'The Show,' and also secured 1st place on 'ENA K-Pop Chart Show.'  After meeting fans through various radio programs, content, and web variety shows since their debut, izna chose 'Weekly Idol' as their first variety program. Having rapidly built their career in less than six months since their official debut, izna is preparing for a comeback in June. With a double celebration of their first variety appearance and comeback, it is anticipated what kind of variety sense and chemistry izna will showcase through 'Weekly Idol.'  Meanwhile, the reorganized 'Weekly Idol' will air for the first time on June 6 at 5:30 p.m. on MBC Every1 and at 11:30 p.m. on MBC M.

classified.text: The real reason rock is no more — I saw some thoughtful posts about how it “goes against the rock ethos” to promote yourself via TikTok, and other posts about how “social media” is inherently individualistic rather than group oriented.  While I agree with these thoughtful points, you know what the bigger reason is?  I’ll spell it out for you.  The time period of let’s say 1940s - 2010 was a time of television, and of English-language dominated culture. All films and music came out of either the United States, or England, with English, western audiences in mind.  This, all in all, made popular music a very “white” affair. I’m talking about race. There was “black” music too, but it wasn’t “African” music. It was “Black American, English-speaking music.” Both white and black music from America and the UK were made from and for the perspective of for lack of a better term, “Anglospheric audiences.” The expression isn’t adequate because that fantastic black music was certainly distinct from white music, but hopefully you see my point.  What social media did is globalize culture. You now have media and communication to hear the voices of people from all over the world. In particular, the Spanish-speaking world and Eastern (Asian) world tends to in many ways dwarf the English speaking world on social media, but this also goes to any culture, like Russians who also have the same social media.   So now, “content” is made with the desires and tastes, the ethos of “other countries” at least partially in mind. The thing is though, other countries (non English-speaking countries) didn’t ever make rock music that frankly matters on any serious level. They may be fans of it, but they didn’t make it, and they won’t in the future (no more than will whites, who also stopped making great rock music).  People in other cultures like Latin America don’t resonate with and never could understand the subtle nuances portrayed in an Ethos like that of Fleetwood Mac, or the lyrics in songs by The Doors, Black Sabbath or Soundgarden. They are more attracted to people like Kim Kardashian, and unfortunately people like Megan the Stallion. These are people who lack taste, and class. Which are unique to western culture, not indigenous cultures. Artists who are loud, in your face and lack panache, drive sports cars, flash gold chains, have grills and face tattoos, and a legion of women with fake breasts and bbls (things that would never appeal to a white, English-speaking only ethos) wind up making a buzz on social media, and attract the biggest amounts of followers, and thus center themselves in the global conversation. In the past, the global conversation was centred around the white male groups and also female artists. Being at the height of the global conversation is what drove and always will drive the greatest artists. It is central to creating pop music to be at the top, the center.  So, you can knock it, or cry all you want, but if you liked the music of people like The Beatles, or Nirvana, I can hope you understand why it is that this music is no more.  To put it real simple, and to be a little less generous in softening the blow, white men are not celebrated on the global stage at all. Everything has been made all about black people, brown people, and queers. Queers used to be a lot more interesting when they had to hide it. Look at Queen, look at George Michael. Of course they came out the closet, but people only cared AFTER the fact that they had created these captivating personas with their insane talent, and proven themselves. Nowadays new artists try to build their whole career off of just being queer, and what happens? A lot of the time no one  gives a fuck.  This type of nuanced conversation will be attacked by the very left leaning music community, but I am 1000% correct in this assessment. It’s a failure of modern culture that these obvious facts can’t be stated in public without being admonished, and I haven’t even pressed Post yet. All it does is prove my point to the moon and back. So go on and attack.  TLDR: The global audience of foreign speaking and foreign-culture values via social media has created a climate where white culture cannot thrive in the way it previously did. The only people who really thrive artistically today are POC, queer, or face-tatted comically goofy looking white men like Post Malone or Jelly Roll. Country music has been the only refuge for traditionally masculine white men, and most of that is beer commercial music, not the Great White Rock of old
